message.content:
Adversaries may exploit Internet Information Services (IIS) web servers by installing malicious components to maintain persistence and manipulate web traffic. IIS supports extensions and filters, such as ISAPI extensions and filters, which can be deployed as DLL files to examine or modify incoming and outgoing web requests. These components can be abused to observe traffic, execute commands, or proxy command and control traffic. IIS modules, introduced in IIS 7.0, offer similar capabilities and can be written as DLLs or .NET applications. Notable examples include IceApple, an IIS post-exploitation framework, and RGDoor, which establishes persistence as an IIS module. The potential impact includes unauthorized access, data manipulation, and distribution of malicious content.

Common adversary tactics include installing ISAPI extensions/filters or IIS modules to gain unrestricted access to HTTP requests and responses. Mitigation strategies involve regular monitoring and auditing of IIS components, employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized DLLs, and ensuring timely updates and patches to IIS and related software. Additionally, implementing robust network segmentation and employing intrusion detection systems can help identify and mitigate such threats.
